But the problem might want ALL violations. Let me assume we want first violation per starting index. So final: [(0,4,1)]

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Iterate through all possible subarrays or intervals to check ad frequency constraints, using nested loops to count occurrences and enforce caps. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs, suitable only for small datasets or as a baseline.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force O(n²) Solution for Ad Frequency Capping
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking all subarrays for frequency constraints using nested loops.
ladders.explanation:
This is the complete brute force solution to the ad frequency capping problem. The intuition is straightforward: we need to examine every possible contiguous subarray (interval) in the ad impression sequence to determine if it violates the frequency cap constraints. Since we're using a brute force approach, we systematically generate all subarrays using two nested loops - an outer loop for the starting index and an inner loop for the ending index. For each subarray, we count the frequency of each ad ID within that interval and check if any ad appears more than the allowed cap. The algorithm works because it exhaustively checks every possible interval, guaranteeing we'll find any violation if it exists. However, this comes at the cost of O(n²) time complexity, making it impractical for large inputs but perfectly suitable for small datasets or as a baseline implementation.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize result variables to track violations
2. Outer loop: For each starting index i from 0 to n-1
3. Initialize a frequency counter (dictionary/hash map)
4. Inner loop: For each ending index j from i to n-1
5. Add ad at position j to frequency counter
6. Check if any ad in counter exceeds frequency cap
7. If violation found, record it and potentially break
8. Continue until all subarrays checked

Time complexity: O(n²) - For each of n starting positions, we examine up to n ending positions
Space complexity: O(k) where k is number of unique ads in current subarray, but O(1) in worst case if we consider alphabet size constant

Connection to L1: This full solution directly uses the subarray frequency checking subroutine from L1 as its core component.

Common mistakes:
1. Off-by-one errors in loop boundaries
2. Forgetting to reset frequency counter for each new starting index
3. Not handling empty input cases
4. Inefficient frequency checking within inner loop

Real code pattern:
def check_frequency_caps(ads, cap):
    n = len(ads)
    violations = []
    for i in range(n):
        freq = {}
        for j in range(i, n):
            ad_id = ads[j]
            freq[ad_id] = freq.get(ad_id, 0) + 1
            if freq[ad_id] > cap:
                violations.append((i, j, ad_id))
                break  # optional optimization
    return violations

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Checking Frequency Caps in a Growing Subarray
ladders.desc: Maintain frequency counts while expanding a subarray and detect when any ad exceeds cap.
ladders.explanation:
This is the core subroutine of the brute force solution. The intuition is that as we expand a subarray by adding elements one by one, we need to efficiently track how many times each ad ID appears and immediately detect when any ad exceeds the frequency cap. This subroutine handles a single starting index and expands to the right, which forms the inner loop of the complete O(n²) solution. The key insight is that we can maintain a frequency dictionary/hash map that gets updated incrementally as we add each new element, rather than recounting from scratch each time. This incremental approach is more efficient than recounting the entire subarray for each new ending position.

Step-by-step algorithm for a fixed starting index i:
1. Initialize empty frequency dictionary
2. For ending index j from i to n-1:
3.   Get ad ID at position j
4.   Increment its count in frequency dictionary
5.   Check if this count exceeds the cap
6.   If yes, record violation (i, j, ad_id) and optionally break
7.   Continue to next j

Time complexity: O(n) for each starting position (worst case)
Space complexity: O(k) where k is number of unique ads in current subarray

Connection to L0: This subroutine is called n times (for each starting position) in the complete solution, forming the O(n²) algorithm.

Connection to L2: This builds upon the basic frequency counting concept from L2 by adding the constraint checking logic.

Common mistakes:
1. Not initializing fresh frequency dictionary for each new starting index
2. Off-by-one errors when accessing array indices
3. Forgetting to handle the case where ad ID is not yet in dictionary
4. Inefficient cap checking (checking all entries instead of just the updated one)

Real code pattern:
def check_from_start(ads, cap, start_idx):
    freq = {}
    violations = []
    for end_idx in range(start_idx, len(ads)):
        ad_id = ads[end_idx]
        freq[ad_id] = freq.get(ad_id, 0) + 1
        if freq[ad_id] > cap:
            violations.append((start_idx, end_idx, ad_id))
            break  # optional
    return violations

end_idx=2: ad=1, freq={1:2,2:1}, check: 2 > 1 → VIOLATION! Record (0,2,1)
Break (optional)

end_idx=2: freq[5]=2 ≤ 3

No violation! Output should be []
But if we continue to index 4 (if it existed), then we'd get violation.
So for this input with n=4, output is []

ladders.title: Core Logic — Frequency Counting in a Contiguous Subarray
ladders.desc: Count occurrences of each element within a specified array interval.
ladders.explanation:
This is the fundamental operation at the heart of frequency capping. The intuition is simple: to check if any ad appears too frequently in a given time interval (subarray), we first need to know how many times each ad ID appears in that interval. This involves iterating through all elements in the specified range and maintaining counts for each unique value. The core insight is that we need a data structure (typically a dictionary or hash map) that maps ad IDs to their occurrence counts, allowing O(1) updates and lookups. This frequency counting is a basic but essential pattern in many algorithms involving frequency constraints or distribution analysis.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize empty frequency dictionary
2. For each index in the specified range [start, end]:
3.   Get the element (ad ID) at that position
4.   If ad ID not in dictionary, add it with count 1
5.   If ad ID already in dictionary, increment its count
6. Return the frequency dictionary

Time complexity: O(m) where m is length of subarray (end-start+1)
Space complexity: O(k) where k is number of unique elements in subarray

Connection to L1: This frequency counting is the first part of the L1 subroutine, which then adds the cap checking logic.

Connection to L3: This builds upon the basic iteration concept from L3 by adding the counting logic.

Common mistakes:
1. Using wrong loop boundaries (off-by-one errors)
2. Not handling the case when element is first encountered
3. Using inefficient data structures (like list search instead of dictionary)
4. Modifying the frequency dictionary incorrectly

Real code pattern:
def count_frequencies(arr, start, end):
    freq = {}
    for i in range(start, end + 1):
        element = arr[i]
        if element in freq:
            freq[element] += 1
        else:
            freq[element] = 1
    return freq

# Alternative with get():
def count_frequencies(arr, start, end):
    freq = {}
    for i in range(start, end + 1):
        element = arr[i]
        freq[element] = freq.get(element, 0) + 1
    return freq

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through All Subarrays of an Array
ladders.desc: Generate all possible contiguous subarrays using nested loops.
ladders.explanation:
This is a fundamental algorithmic pattern that forms the backbone of the brute force approach. The intuition is that to examine every possible time interval (contiguous sequence) in the ad impression log, we need to systematically generate all subarrays. A subarray is defined by its starting and ending indices, and we need to consider every possible pair where start ≤ end. The most straightforward way to do this is with two nested loops: the outer loop controls the starting index, and the inner loop controls the ending index. For each starting position i, we consider all ending positions j where j ≥ i. This generates all possible subarrays exactly once.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Let n = length of array
2. For i from 0 to n-1 (starting index):
3.   For j from i to n-1 (ending index):
4.     Process subarray from i to j (inclusive)

Time complexity: O(n²) - There are n choices for i, and for each i, up to n choices for j
Space complexity: O(1) - We're just generating indices, not storing all subarrays

Why this works: This exhaustive approach guarantees we consider every possible contiguous subsequence. For an array of length n, there are n(n+1)/2 possible subarrays, which this algorithm generates efficiently.

Connection to L2: This subarray generation provides the intervals that L2 then processes to count frequencies.

Connection to L4: This builds upon basic loop iteration from L4 by adding the nested structure.

Common mistakes:
1. Starting inner loop at 0 instead of i (generates non-contiguous sequences)
2. Using wrong loop boundaries (should be n-1, not n)
3. Not understanding that subarrays must be contiguous
4. Trying to store all subarrays (wasting memory)

Real code pattern:
def generate_all_subarrays(arr):
    n = len(arr)
    for i in range(n):           # start index
        for j in range(i, n):    # end index
            # Current subarray is arr[i:j+1]
            # Process it here
            pass

examples.trace:
n = 3
i=0:
  j=0: subarray [1]
  j=1: subarray [1,2]
  j=2: subarray [1,2,3]
i=1:
  j=1: subarray [2]
  j=2: subarray [2,3]
i=2:
  j=2: subarray [3]
Total 6 subarrays = 3*4/2 = 6

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Traversal with Index Tracking
ladders.desc: Iterate through array elements while keeping track of current position.
ladders.explanation:
This is one of the most fundamental operations in programming - sequentially accessing each element in an array while knowing its position. The intuition is simple: arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and we often need to process each element in order. The key concept is using an index variable (typically i, j, or idx) that starts at 0 (the first element in most languages) and increments by 1 each step until it reaches the end of the array. This allows us to both access the element value and know its position in the array, which is crucial for algorithms that need positional information like subarray generation.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize index variable to 0
2. While index is less than array length:
3.   Access element at array[index]
4.   Process the element
5.   Increment index by 1
6. Repeat until all elements processed

Time complexity: O(n) where n is array length
Space complexity: O(1) - only storing the index variable

Why this matters: Almost all array algorithms build upon this basic pattern. In frequency capping, we need to traverse arrays to count occurrences, generate subarrays, and check constraints.

Connection to L3: This basic traversal is the building block for the nested loops in L3 that generate all subarrays.

Connection to L5: This applies the concept of iteration from L5 to the specific context of arrays.

Common mistakes:
1. Starting index at 1 instead of 0
2. Using <= instead of < for loop condition (causing index out of bounds)
3. Forgetting to increment the index (infinite loop)
4. Modifying the array while iterating (unless intentional)

Real code pattern:
# Using for loop (most common):
for i in range(len(arr)):
    element = arr[i]
    # process element

# Using while loop:
i = 0
while i < len(arr):
    element = arr[i]
    # process element
    i += 1

examples.trace:
len(arr) = 3
Using for loop with range(3):
i=0: arr[0]=10
i=1: arr[1]=20
i=2: arr[2]=30
Done

len(arr) = 3
i=0: arr[0]=5
i=1: arr[1]=5
i=2: arr[2]=5
Done

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Basic Iteration/Looping
ladders.desc: Repeating an action multiple times with a counter.
ladders.explanation:
This is the absolute foundation of the entire algorithm - the concept of repeating an action multiple times. In programming, this is done using loops. The intuition is that many problems require us to perform the same operation on multiple items or until a condition is met. A loop has three essential components: initialization (starting point), condition (when to continue), and update (how to move to next iteration). The most basic loop is the for loop, which typically repeats a fixed number of times, and the while loop, which repeats as long as a condition is true. Understanding loops is prerequisite to any algorithm that processes multiple data elements.

For a simple counter-based loop:
1. Initialize a counter variable (e.g., i = 0)
2. Check if counter meets continuation condition (e.g., i < 5)
3. If true, execute loop body
4. Update counter (e.g., i = i + 1)
5. Go back to step 2

Why this matters: All array processing, including the O(n²) brute force solution, relies on loops. The outer and inner loops in our algorithm are just applications of this fundamental concept.

Connection to L4: This basic looping concept is applied specifically to array traversal in L4.

Common mistakes beginners make:
1. Forgetting to initialize the counter
2. Creating infinite loops by not updating the counter
3. Using wrong comparison operators (<= vs <)
4. Not understanding that loop indices typically start at 0

Real code pattern:
# Print numbers 0 to 4
for i in range(5):
    print(i)

# Equivalent while loop:
i = 0
while i < 5:
    print(i)
    i += 1

examples.trace:
Using for i in range(3):
First iteration: i=0, print 0
Second iteration: i=1, print 1
Third iteration: i=2, print 2
Done because range(3) gives 0,1,2

Using for i in range(1, 4):
range(1,4) gives 1,2,3
First: i=1, print 1
Second: i=2, print 2
Third: i=3, print 3
Done

approaches.name: Chinese Remainder Theorem O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Use the Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT) to solve modular equations derived from ad frequency constraints, reducing the problem to finding solutions modulo pairwise coprime moduli. This approach efficiently handles frequency caps by transforming them into a system of congruences.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Ad Frequency Capping via CRT
ladders.desc: Complete solution using Chinese Remainder Theorem to solve ad frequency constraints as modular equations.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires finding the smallest non-negative integer X (ad display count) that satisfies multiple frequency cap constraints of the form X ≡ r_i (mod m_i), where each constraint means 'after every m_i ads, at most r_i ads of this type can be shown'. The Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT) provides an efficient solution when all moduli are pairwise coprime. The intuition is that each constraint creates a congruence equation, and CRT combines them into a single solution modulo the product of all moduli. This works because the constraints are independent when moduli are coprime, allowing us to find a unique solution modulo M = m_1 * m_2 * ... * m_n. The algorithm: 1) Verify all moduli are pairwise coprime (if not, the problem may have no solution). 2) Compute M = product of all moduli. 3) For each constraint (r_i, m_i), compute M_i = M / m_i. 4) Find the modular inverse of M_i modulo m_i (call it inv_i). 5) The solution is X = Σ(r_i * M_i * inv_i) mod M. 6) Return the smallest non-negative solution. Time complexity is O(n log max(m_i)) due to modular inverse computations using extended Euclidean algorithm. Space is O(n) for storing intermediate values. This connects to L1 by using the CRT solver as the key subroutine. Common mistakes: forgetting to check coprimality, mishandling negative remainders, or not reducing intermediate results properly. Real code pattern: def solve_frequency_caps(constraints): # constraints = list of (r_i, m_i) pairs; M = 1; for _, m in constraints: M *= m; result = 0; for r, m in constraints: M_i = M // m; inv = mod_inverse(M_i, m); result = (result + r * M_i * inv) % M; return result.

examples.trace: Constraints: X ≡ 2 (mod 3), X ≡ 3 (mod 5), X ≡ 2 (mod 7). All moduli (3,5,7) are pairwise coprime. Step 1: M = 3*5*7 = 105. Step 2: For (2,3): M_1 = 105/3 = 35, inv_1 = modular inverse of 35 mod 3. 35 mod 3 = 2, inverse of 2 mod 3 is 2 since 2*2=4≡1 mod 3. Term1 = 2*35*2 = 140. Step 3: For (3,5): M_2 = 105/5 = 21, inv_2 = inverse of 21 mod 5. 21 mod 5 = 1, inverse of 1 mod 5 is 1. Term2 = 3*21*1 = 63. Step 4: For (2,7): M_3 = 105/7 = 15, inv_3 = inverse of 15 mod 7. 15 mod 7 = 1, inverse of 1 mod 7 is 1. Term3 = 2*15*1 = 30. Step 5: X = (140+63+30) mod 105 = 233 mod 105 = 23. Step 6: Verify: 23 mod 3 = 2, 23 mod 5 = 3, 23 mod 7 = 2. All satisfied.

Constraints: X ≡ 1 (mod 2), X ≡ 2 (mod 3), X ≡ 3 (mod 5). Moduli 2,3,5 are coprime. Step 1: M = 2*3*5 = 30. Step 2: For (1,2): M_1 = 30/2 = 15, inv_1 = inverse of 15 mod 2. 15 mod 2 = 1, inverse is 1. Term1 = 1*15*1 = 15. Step 3: For (2,3): M_2 = 30/3 = 10, inv_2 = inverse of 10 mod 3. 10 mod 3 = 1, inverse is 1. Term2 = 2*10*1 = 20. Step 4: For (3,5): M_3 = 30/5 = 6, inv_3 = inverse of 6 mod 5. 6 mod 5 = 1, inverse is 1. Term3 = 3*6*1 = 18. Step 5: X = (15+20+18) mod 30 = 53 mod 30 = 23. But wait, 53 mod 30 = 23, but verify: 23 mod 2 = 1, 23 mod 3 = 2, 23 mod 5 = 3. Correct. Actually 53 mod 30 = 23, so output is 23. Let me recalc: 15+20+18=53, 53 mod 30 = 23. Yes, output 23. Correction: output is 23, not 53. Trace correction: Step 5: Sum = 53, X = 53 mod 30 = 23. Verify: 23 mod 2 = 1, 23 mod 3 = 2, 23 mod 5 = 3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — CRT Solver for Pairwise Coprime Moduli
ladders.desc: Implement the Chinese Remainder Theorem to solve a system of congruences X ≡ r_i (mod m_i).
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine solves the core mathematical problem: given k congruences X ≡ r_i (mod m_i) with pairwise coprime moduli m_i, find the smallest non-negative X that satisfies all. The intuition is that CRT guarantees a unique solution modulo M = product of all m_i. The algorithm works by constructing the solution as a weighted sum: X = Σ (r_i * M_i * inv_i) mod M, where M_i = M/m_i and inv_i is the modular inverse of M_i modulo m_i. This formula works because each term r_i * M_i * inv_i is congruent to r_i modulo m_i (since M_i * inv_i ≡ 1 mod m_i) and congruent to 0 modulo all other m_j (since M_i contains all other moduli as factors). Step-by-step: 1) Check all moduli are pairwise coprime (return error if not). 2) Compute M = m_1 * m_2 * ... * m_k. 3) Initialize result = 0. 4) For each i: compute M_i = M / m_i; compute inv_i = modular_inverse(M_i, m_i) using extended Euclidean algorithm; add r_i * M_i * inv_i to result. 5) Return result mod M. Time complexity: O(k log max(m_i)) for k congruences, dominated by modular inverse computations. Space: O(1) extra space. This feeds into L0 as the main solver for frequency cap constraints. Common mistakes: not handling large numbers (use modular arithmetic throughout), assuming moduli are always coprime, or incorrect inverse computation. Real code pattern: def crt_solver(constraints): M = 1; for _, m in constraints: M *= m; result = 0; for r, m in constraints: M_i = M // m; inv = mod_inverse(M_i, m); result = (result + r * M_i * inv) % M; return result.

examples.trace: Constraints: X ≡ 2 (mod 3), X ≡ 3 (mod 5). Moduli 3 and 5 are coprime. Step 1: M = 3*5 = 15. Step 2: For (2,3): M_1 = 15/3 = 5, inv_1 = inverse of 5 mod 3. 5 mod 3 = 2, inverse of 2 mod 3 is 2 since 2*2=4≡1 mod 3. Term1 = 2*5*2 = 20. Step 3: For (3,5): M_2 = 15/5 = 3, inv_2 = inverse of 3 mod 5. 3 mod 5 = 3, inverse of 3 mod 5 is 2 since 3*2=6≡1 mod 5. Term2 = 3*3*2 = 18. Step 4: X = (20+18) mod 15 = 38 mod 15 = 8. Step 5: Verify: 8 mod 3 = 2, 8 mod 5 = 3.

Constraints: X ≡ 1 (mod 4), X ≡ 3 (mod 5). Moduli 4 and 5 coprime. Step 1: M = 4*5 = 20. Step 2: For (1,4): M_1 = 20/4 = 5, inv_1 = inverse of 5 mod 4. 5 mod 4 = 1, inverse of 1 mod 4 is 1. Term1 = 1*5*1 = 5. Step 3: For (3,5): M_2 = 20/5 = 4, inv_2 = inverse of 4 mod 5. 4 mod 5 = 4, inverse of 4 mod 5 is 4 since 4*4=16≡1 mod 5. Term2 = 3*4*4 = 48. Step 4: X = (5+48) mod 20 = 53 mod 20 = 13. Wait, 53 mod 20 = 13, but verify: 13 mod 4 = 1, 13 mod 5 = 3. Correct, output 13. Correction: output is 13, not 11. Trace correction: Step 4: Sum = 53, X = 53 mod 20 = 13.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Modular Inverse via Extended Euclidean Algorithm
ladders.desc: Compute the modular inverse of a modulo m using the extended Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The modular inverse of an integer a modulo m is an integer x such that a*x ≡ 1 (mod m). It exists only if gcd(a, m) = 1. The extended Euclidean algorithm computes both gcd(a, m) and coefficients x, y such that a*x + m*y = gcd(a, m). When gcd(a, m) = 1, this becomes a*x + m*y = 1, so modulo m we have a*x ≡ 1 (mod m), making x the modular inverse of a modulo m (taking x mod m to ensure it's in range 0..m-1). The intuition is that the algorithm recursively finds gcd and back-substitutes to express it as a linear combination. Step-by-step: 1) If m = 0, return (a, 1, 0) as (gcd, x, y). 2) Otherwise, recursively call extended_gcd(m, a mod m) to get (gcd, x1, y1). 3) Then x = y1, y = x1 - (a // m) * y1. 4) For modular inverse: call extended_gcd(a, m); if gcd != 1, no inverse exists; else return x mod m. Time complexity: O(log min(a, m)) due to recursive reduction. Space: O(log min(a, m)) for recursion stack. This feeds into L1 as it's needed for computing inverses in CRT. Common mistakes: not checking gcd=1, not taking result modulo m, or mishandling negative x. Real code pattern: def mod_inverse(a, m): g, x, y = extended_gcd(a, m); if g != 1: return None; else: return x % m. def extended_gcd(a, b): if b == 0: return (a, 1, 0); else: g, x1, y1 = extended_gcd(b, a % b); x = y1; y = x1 - (a // b) * y1; return (g, x, y).

examples.trace: Compute inverse of 3 mod 11. Step 1: extended_gcd(3, 11). Since 11 != 0, call extended_gcd(11, 3 mod 11 = 3). Step 2: extended_gcd(11, 3). Since 3 != 0, call extended_gcd(3, 11 mod 3 = 2). Step 3: extended_gcd(3, 2). Since 2 != 0, call extended_gcd(2, 3 mod 2 = 1). Step 4: extended_gcd(2, 1). Since 1 != 0, call extended_gcd(1, 2 mod 1 = 0). Step 5: extended_gcd(1, 0). Base case: return (1, 1, 0). Now back-substitute: For call (2,1): g=1, x1=1, y1=0 from child; x = y1 = 0; y = x1 - (2//1)*y1 = 1 - 2*0 = 1; return (1, 0, 1). For call (3,2): g=1, x1=0, y1=1; x = y1 = 1; y = x1 - (3//2)*y1 = 0 - 1*1 = -1; return (1, 1, -1). For call (11,3): g=1, x1=1, y1=-1; x = y1 = -1; y = x1 - (11//3)*y1 = 1 - 3*(-1) = 4; return (1, -1, 4). For call (3,11): g=1, x1=-1, y1=4; x = y1 = 4; y = x1 - (3//11)*y1 = -1 - 0*4 = -1; return (1, 4, -1). Step 6: g=1, so inverse exists. x = 4 mod 11 = 4. Verify: 3*4 = 12 ≡ 1 mod 11.

Compute inverse of 5 mod 12. Step 1: extended_gcd(5, 12). Call extended_gcd(12, 5 mod 12 = 5). Step 2: extended_gcd(12, 5). Call extended_gcd(5, 12 mod 5 = 2). Step 3: extended_gcd(5, 2). Call extended_gcd(2, 5 mod 2 = 1). Step 4: extended_gcd(2, 1). Call extended_gcd(1, 2 mod 1 = 0). Step 5: extended_gcd(1, 0): return (1, 1, 0). Back-substitute: For (2,1): x1=1, y1=0; x=0, y=1 - (2//1)*0=1; return (1,0,1). For (5,2): x1=0, y1=1; x=1, y=0 - (5//2)*1 = -2; return (1,1,-2). For (12,5): x1=1, y1=-2; x=-2, y=1 - (12//5)*(-2) = 1 - 2*(-2)=5; return (1,-2,5). For (5,12): x1=-2, y1=5; x=5, y=-2 - (5//12)*5 = -2 - 0= -2; return (1,5,-2). Step 6: g=1, inverse exists. x = 5 mod 12 = 5. Verify: 5*5=25 ≡ 1 mod 12.

ladders.title: Building Block — Extended Euclidean Algorithm for GCD and Coefficients
ladders.desc: Implement the extended Euclidean algorithm to compute gcd(a,b) and integers x,y such that a*x + b*y = gcd(a,b).
ladders.explanation: The extended Euclidean algorithm computes the greatest common divisor (gcd) of two integers a and b, along with coefficients x and y (Bézout coefficients) that satisfy Bézout's identity: a*x + b*y = gcd(a,b). The intuition follows from the property that gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b). By recursively applying this and back-substituting, we can express the gcd as a linear combination. The algorithm: 1) Base case: if b = 0, return (a, 1, 0) since a*1 + 0*0 = a. 2) Recursive case: compute (g, x1, y1) = extended_gcd(b, a mod b). 3) Then update: x = y1, y = x1 - (a // b) * y1. 4) Return (g, x, y). This works because if we have b*x1 + (a mod b)*y1 = g, and a mod b = a - (a//b)*b, substituting gives a*y1 + b*(x1 - (a//b)*y1) = g. Time complexity: O(log min(a,b)) same as standard Euclidean algorithm. Space: O(log min(a,b)) for recursion. This feeds into L2 as it's used to find modular inverses. Common mistakes: incorrect base case, wrong sign in update, or not handling negative numbers properly. Real code pattern: def extended_gcd(a, b): if b == 0: return (a, 1, 0); else: g, x1, y1 = extended_gcd(b, a % b); x = y1; y = x1 - (a // b) * y1; return (g, x, y).

examples.trace: Compute extended_gcd(30, 20). Step 1: b=20 != 0, so call extended_gcd(20, 30 mod 20 = 10). Step 2: extended_gcd(20, 10). b=10 != 0, call extended_gcd(10, 20 mod 10 = 0). Step 3: extended_gcd(10, 0). Base case: return (10, 1, 0). Back to step 2: g=10, x1=1, y1=0; x = y1 = 0; y = x1 - (20//10)*y1 = 1 - 2*0 = 1; return (10, 0, 1). Back to step 1: g=10, x1=0, y1=1; x = y1 = 1; y = x1 - (30//20)*y1 = 0 - 1*1 = -1; return (10, 1, -1). Verify: 30*1 + 20*(-1) = 30 - 20 = 10 = gcd(30,20).

Compute extended_gcd(14, 21). Step 1: b=21 != 0, call extended_gcd(21, 14 mod 21 = 14). Step 2: extended_gcd(21, 14). b=14 != 0, call extended_gcd(14, 21 mod 14 = 7). Step 3: extended_gcd(14, 7). b=7 != 0, call extended_gcd(7, 14 mod 7 = 0). Step 4: extended_gcd(7, 0). Base case: return (7, 1, 0). Back to step 3: g=7, x1=1, y1=0; x = y1 = 0; y = x1 - (14//7)*y1 = 1 - 2*0 = 1; return (7, 0, 1). Back to step 2: g=7, x1=0, y1=1; x = y1 = 1; y = x1 - (21//14)*y1 = 0 - 1*1 = -1; return (7, 1, -1). Back to step 1: g=7, x1=1, y1=-1; x = y1 = -1; y = x1 - (14//21)*y1 = 1 - 0*(-1) = 1; return (7, -1, 1). Verify: 14*(-1) + 21*1 = -14 + 21 = 7 = gcd(14,21).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Euclidean Algorithm for GCD
ladders.desc: Compute the greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers using the Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The Euclidean algorithm finds the greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers a and b efficiently. The intuition is based on the property that gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b). By repeatedly replacing (a,b) with (b, a mod b) until b becomes 0, the GCD is the non-zero remainder. This works because any common divisor of a and b also divides a mod b, and vice versa. Step-by-step iterative algorithm: 1) While b != 0: set temp = b; b = a mod b; a = temp. 2) Return a. Alternatively, recursive: if b == 0, return a; else return gcd(b, a mod b). Time complexity: O(log min(a,b)) since the numbers reduce quickly (at least halved every two steps). Space: O(1) iterative, O(log min(a,b)) recursive. This feeds into L3 as the foundation for the extended version. Common mistakes: not handling negative numbers (take absolute values), infinite loop if not updating correctly, or recursion depth issues for large numbers. Real code pattern: def gcd(a, b): while b: a, b = b, a % b; return abs(a).

examples.trace: Compute gcd(48, 18). Step 1: b=18 != 0, so set temp=18, b = 48 mod 18 = 12, a = 18. Now a=18, b=12. Step 2: b=12 != 0, temp=12, b = 18 mod 12 = 6, a=12. Now a=12, b=6. Step 3: b=6 != 0, temp=6, b = 12 mod 6 = 0, a=6. Step 4: b=0, so return a=6. Verify: divisors of 48: 1,2,3,4,6,8,12,16,24,48; of 18: 1,2,3,6,9,18; common: 1,2,3,6; greatest is 6.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Modular Arithmetic Basics
ladders.desc: Understand modulo operation and basic properties like congruence and remainders.
ladders.explanation: Modular arithmetic deals with integers and a modulus m, where we consider numbers equivalent if they differ by a multiple of m. The modulo operation a mod m gives the remainder when a is divided by m, always in range 0 ≤ r < m. Key concepts: 1) Congruence: a ≡ b (mod m) means m divides (a - b). 2) Properties: (a mod m) + (b mod m) mod m = (a+b) mod m; similarly for subtraction and multiplication. Division is not straightforward—it requires modular inverses. The intuition is that modulo wraps numbers around a circle of size m. For example, with m=12, it's like clock arithmetic. Step-by-step for computing a mod m: if a ≥ 0, divide a by m, remainder is a mod m; if a < 0, add multiples of m until non-negative (e.g., -3 mod 5 = 2). Time complexity: O(1) for basic operations. Space: O(1). This feeds into L4 as GCD uses modulo. Common mistakes: confusing remainder with quotient, mishandling negative numbers, or not reducing results modulo m in intermediate steps. Real code pattern: In Python, a % m gives remainder with sign of divisor; for non-negative remainder, use (a % m + m) % m if a might be negative.

examples.trace: Step 1: Divide 17 by 5. 5*3 = 15, remainder = 17 - 15 = 2. Step 2: Since remainder 2 is in range 0 to 4, 17 mod 5 = 2. Verify: 17 ≡ 2 (mod 5) because 17 - 2 = 15, which is divisible by 5.

Step 1: For negative numbers, add the modulus until non-negative. Start with -8. Add 3: -8 + 3 = -5. Add 3 again: -5 + 3 = -2. Add 3 again: -2 + 3 = 1. Step 2: Now 1 is in range 0 to 2, so -8 mod 3 = 1. Alternatively, compute: -8 divided by 3 gives quotient -3 (since 3*(-3) = -9, remainder = -8 - (-9) = 1). Verify: -8 ≡ 1 (mod 3) because -8 - 1 = -9, divisible by 3.

approaches.name: Greedy with Priority Queue O(n log k)
approaches.summary: Maintain a priority queue (min-heap) to track ad slots and their next available times based on frequency caps, greedily assigning ads to the earliest available slots. This optimizes scheduling by minimizing delays and ensuring constraints are met dynamically.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Ad Frequency Capping via CRT with Greedy Priority Queue
ladders.desc: Complete solution using greedy approach with min-heap to schedule ads while respecting frequency caps.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires scheduling ads with frequency caps (maximum number of times an ad can be shown within a time window) using a greedy priority queue approach. The intuition is to maintain a min-heap that tracks ad slots and their next available times based on frequency constraints. By always assigning ads to the earliest available slot, we minimize delays while ensuring caps are not violated. This works because it's essentially a scheduling problem where we want to maximize throughput while respecting constraints—the greedy choice of earliest available slot is optimal as it leaves more flexibility for future ads. The algorithm processes ads in order: for each ad, check if it can be scheduled now based on its frequency cap; if not, pop from heap to find next available time, update it, and push back. Time complexity is O(n log k) where n is number of ads and k is number of unique ads (heap size). Space is O(k) for the heap. This connects to L1 as it integrates the key subroutine of heap management. Common mistakes include not properly updating heap entries when caps are hit or mishandling time calculations. Real code pattern: initialize heap, process ads, maintain hashmap for ad counts and times, use heap to find next available slot.

examples.trace: Initialize: heap empty, time = 0, counts = {}. Ad 1: no cap hit, schedule at time 0, update counts[1] = [(0,1)], push (0+window=3,1) to heap, output[0]=0. Ad 2: schedule at time 0, counts[2]=[(0,1)], push (3,2), output[1]=0. Ad 1: count=1, check heap top (3,1) > current time 0? No, so cap not hit yet, but need to check if within window: last time for ad 1 was 0, current time 0, difference 0 < window 3, and count 1 < cap 2, so schedule at time 0? Wait, actually we need to ensure not exceeding cap within window. Since last time was 0 and window is 3, we have 1 occurrence in last 3 time units, so can schedule at time 0. But let's trace properly: For ad 1 at index 2, current time is 0 (since we schedule greedily). Check heap: top is (3,1) meaning next available after 3. But we're at time 0, so we can schedule if count < cap. Count for ad 1 is 1, cap is 2, so schedule at time 0, update counts to 2, push new entry (0+3=3,1) but heap already has (3,1), so we need to manage properly. Actually, we should maintain heap for next available times, and when scheduling, if current time >= heap top for that ad, we can schedule and update heap. Let's re-trace with correct algorithm: Initialize heap empty, time = 0. Ad 1: heap has no entry for ad 1, so schedule at time 0, output[0]=0, push (0+3=3,1) to heap. Ad 2: heap has no entry for ad 2, schedule at time 0, output[1]=0, push (3,2). Ad 1: heap top is (3,1), current time is 0, since 0 < 3, we cannot schedule at time 0 because next available is 3. So we need to pop and schedule at time 3? But we want to minimize delay, so we pop (3,1), schedule at time 3, output[2]=3, push new next available time 3+3=6. Ad 2: heap top is (3,2), current time is 3, since 3 >= 3, we can schedule at time 3, output[3]=3, push new time 6. Final output [0,0,3,3].

Initialize heap empty, time = 0. Ad 1: schedule at 0, output[0]=0, push (0+2=2,1). Ad 1: heap top (2,1), current time 0 < 2, so cannot schedule at 0. Pop (2,1), schedule at time 2, output[1]=2, push new time 4. Ad 1: heap top (4,1), current time 2 < 4, pop, schedule at 4, output[2]=4, push time 6. Ad 2: heap has no entry for 2, schedule at current time 4? But we want to schedule as early as possible, so since no constraint, schedule at time 0? Actually, we process in order, so for ad 2 at index 3, current time is 4 from previous ad. But we can schedule it earlier if possible. To minimize overall schedule, we should consider time. Let's use algorithm: maintain current time as max of last scheduled time. For ad 2, current time is 4, heap has no entry, so schedule at time max(4,0)=4? But we can schedule at time 0 since no cap yet. However, to keep order, we schedule at current time. But in greedy, we schedule at earliest available, which for ad 2 is time 0. So we need to adjust: we keep a global time that advances as we schedule. Actually, let's trace correctly: Start time = 0. Ad 1: schedule at 0, time = 0, push (2,1). Ad 1: heap top (2,1), time=0 < 2, so pop, schedule at time 2, time=2, push (4,1). Ad 1: heap top (4,1), time=2 < 4, pop, schedule at 4, time=4, push (6,1). Ad 2: heap no entry for 2, so schedule at current time 4? But we can schedule earlier since no cap. To minimize, we schedule at time 0. So we need to set time = max(time, earliest available). For ad 2, earliest available is 0, so schedule at 0, output[3]=0, push (2,2). But then time becomes 0? That would reorder. Actually, we process in sequence, so output times should be non-decreasing? Not necessarily, but for scheduling, we can assign times independently. The problem likely expects schedule times for each ad. So for ad 2, we assign time 0. So output [0,2,4,0]. Yes.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Managing Priority Queue for Ad Scheduling
ladders.desc: The helper function that handles heap operations to determine next available times for ads.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the greedy approach, responsible for managing a min-heap that stores pairs of (next_available_time, ad_id). The intuition is that by keeping the earliest available times at the top of the heap, we can quickly decide when an ad can be scheduled next, ensuring we respect frequency caps without scanning all past occurrences. This works because frequency caps impose a minimum time gap between consecutive showings of the same ad within a window; the heap efficiently tracks these gaps. The algorithm: when scheduling an ad, check if there's an entry for that ad in the heap; if not, it can be scheduled immediately. If there is, compare current time with the heap's top time; if current time is greater or equal, schedule and update the heap with new next available time (current time + window); otherwise, pop the heap to get the next available time, schedule at that time, and push updated time. Time complexity per operation is O(log k) for heap push/pop, where k is heap size. Space is O(k). This connects to L0 as it's the main logic called repeatedly in the full solution. Common mistakes include not popping the heap when current time is less than top time, or incorrectly updating times. Real code pattern: use heapq in Python, maintain heap as list of tuples.

examples.trace: Heap initially has (3,1) and (5,2). For ad_id=1, we find entry (3,1) at top. Current_time=2 < top_time=3, so we cannot schedule at current time. Pop (3,1) from heap. Scheduled_time = popped_time = 3. Update next available time for ad 1: scheduled_time + window = 3+3=6. Push (6,1) to heap. Heap now contains (5,2) and (6,1). Output scheduled_time=3.

Heap has (4,2). For ad_id=2, top is (4,2). Current_time=5 >= top_time=4, so we can schedule at current time. Pop (4,2). Scheduled_time = current_time = 5. Update next available time: 5+2=7. Push (7,2) to heap. Heap becomes [(7,2)].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Scheduling with Frequency Constraints
ladders.desc: The central technique of assigning ads to earliest available times while respecting caps.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the greedy scheduling logic that underlies the solution. The intuition is that to minimize overall schedule delay, we should always schedule an ad as early as possible without violating its frequency cap. This is optimal because delaying an ad unnecessarily could cause cascading delays for subsequent ads. The technique involves maintaining a current time pointer and for each ad, determining the earliest time it can be scheduled based on past occurrences and the window constraint. Specifically, for an ad with cap c and window w, it can be scheduled at time t if there have been fewer than c occurrences in the time interval [t-w+1, t]. The greedy approach checks this by tracking last c times for each ad, but the heap optimizes this. The step-by-step: initialize time = 0, for each ad, if count of ad in last w time is less than cap, schedule at current time, else increment time until condition met. With heap, we use next available times. Time complexity O(n * c) without heap, but with heap it's better. Space O(k) for tracking. Connects to L1 as this logic is implemented using the heap subroutine. Common mistakes include not correctly calculating the time window or mishandling when multiple ads compete. Real code pattern: iterate through ads, update counts, adjust time.

examples.trace: Ad 1: count in last 2 time units (from time -1 to 0) is 0, cap is 1, so schedule at time 0, update count to 1, record time 0. Ad 1 again: at current time 0, count in last 2 units (times -1 to 0) is 1, which equals cap, so cannot schedule at time 0. Increment time to 1: check last 2 units (times -1 to 1), count is still 1 (from time 0), so can schedule at time 1? But cap is 1 per window, so need exactly 1 in any window of size 2. At time 1, window [0,1] has 1 occurrence (time 0), so yes can schedule at time 1. So schedule at time 1. But with heap, we'd get time 2. Let's do greedy without heap: actually, to respect cap, after scheduling at time 0, next schedule must be at time >= 2 to have window [1,2] not include time 0. So schedule at time 2. So output [0,2].

Ad 1: schedule at 0, count for ad 1 in last 3 units is 1. Ad 2: schedule at 0, count for ad 2 is 1. Ad 1: at time 0, count for ad 1 in last 3 units is 1, cap is 2, so can schedule at time 0? But need to check if scheduling at time 0 would make count 2 in window [ -2, 0]? Actually, last 3 units from time -2 to 0, after scheduling first ad at 0, count is 1. Scheduling again at 0 would make count 2 in window [ -2, 0], which is allowed since cap is 2. So schedule at time 0. But with heap, we might delay to avoid future issues. Let's use greedy logic: for ad 1 at third position, we have times [0] for ad 1, window size 3, so at time 0, occurrences in [ -2,0] are 1, so can schedule at 0. So output [0,0,0]. But problem might require spacing. Actually, frequency cap means at most c times in any window of w. So for ad 1 with cap 2, window 3, two showings at time 0 are allowed since window [0,2] has 2 showings. So yes, schedule at 0. But in example, output is [0,0,3] from L0, so there's nuance. Let's re-evaluate: the cap is per time window, so if we schedule ad 1 at times 0 and 0, that's two showings at same time, which might be considered same time point? Usually, schedule times are integers, so same time means simultaneous, which may not be allowed. So we need distinct times. So for ad 1, after scheduling at time 0, next must be at time >=1 to have distinct time. But cap allows 2 in window 3, so at time 1, window [ -1,1] has 1 showing, so can schedule at 1. So greedy might schedule at 1. But to minimize, schedule at earliest, so 1. However, in L0 example, they scheduled at 3 due to heap mechanism. This shows the core logic needs careful definition.

ladders.title: Building Block — Frequency Cap Constraint Checking
ladders.desc: Intermediate concept of determining if an ad can be shown at a given time based on past showings and window.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves checking whether scheduling an ad at a specific time would violate its frequency cap constraint. The intuition is that for an ad with cap c and window w, we need to ensure that in any time window of length w ending at the scheduled time, the ad has been shown at most c-1 times before this new showing. This is crucial because it enforces the business rule of not overwhelming users with the same ad. The algorithm: maintain a list of past showing times for the ad; when considering a new time t, count how many showings fall in the interval [t-w+1, t] (inclusive). If count < c, it's allowed; otherwise, not. To optimize, we can keep the list sorted or use a sliding window. Time complexity per check is O(m) where m is number of past showings, but with caps, m is bounded by c. Space O(c) per ad. This connects to L2 as it's the constraint that the greedy scheduling must respect. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in window calculation or not considering the new showing itself in the count. Real code pattern: use deque or list to store times, pop old times outside window.

examples.trace: Past showings at times 0 and 2. New time is 3. Window is 3, so consider interval [3-3+1=1, 3] i.e., [1,3]. Past times in this interval: 2 is in [1,3], 0 is not. So count = 1. Cap is 2, so count < cap, allowed. Include new showing, total would be 2, which is <= cap.

Past times 1 and 4. New time 5. Window 4, interval [5-4+1=2, 5] i.e., [2,5]. Past times in interval: 4 is in [2,5], 1 is not. Count = 1. Cap is 1, so count >= cap, not allowed because adding new would make 2 > cap.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Maintaining a Min-Heap of Time-Key Pairs
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of inserting and extracting minimum from a priority queue.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic operation of using a min-heap (priority queue) to manage pairs of time and ad identifier. The intuition is that a min-heap allows efficient access to the smallest time, which is essential for finding the earliest available slot for ads. This works because heap properties ensure the root is the minimum element, and operations like push and pop maintain this in O(log n) time. The algorithm: represent heap as a list of tuples (time, ad_id). Use heapify to build heap, heappush to insert, heappop to remove minimum. When scheduling, we often need to find and update specific ad entries, which requires additional logic since heaps don't support direct update; we push new entries and ignore old ones. Time complexity: O(log k) per push/pop. Space O(k). This connects to L3 as the heap is used to implement constraint checking efficiently. Common mistakes include not using tuples correctly or misunderstanding heap order. Real code pattern: import heapq, heap = [], heapq.heappush(heap, (time, id)), heapq.heappop(heap).

examples.trace: Initial heap: [(5,1), (3,2), (7,3)]. After heappop, the minimum element is (3,2), so it is removed. Heap reorganizes to maintain min-heap property: becomes [(5,1), (7,3)].
Initial heap: [(2,1)]. After heappush of (4,2), heap becomes [(2,1), (4,2)]. The min-heap property is maintained as 2 < 4.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Frequency Caps and Time Windows
ladders.desc: Simplest prerequisite: what frequency capping means in ad scheduling.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level explains the basic concept of frequency capping in advertising: it's a rule that limits how many times a specific ad can be shown to a user within a given time window. The intuition is to prevent ad fatigue and improve user experience by not overwhelming them with repetitive content. For example, an ad might have a cap of 3 times per hour, meaning a user should see it at most 3 times in any 60-minute period. This involves two key parameters: cap (maximum showings) and window (time duration). The algorithm at this level is simply to count occurrences within sliding time windows. Time complexity depends on implementation but can be O(n) for simple counting. Space O(1) per ad if only counting. This connects to L4 as it's the constraint that more advanced data structures like heaps help enforce. Common mistakes include confusing cap with total showings or misinterpreting window boundaries. Real code pattern: use a counter and time tracker.

examples.trace: First showing at time 0: no previous showings in last 60 min, so allowed. Second at time 30: showings in last 60 min (from -30 to 30) include time 0, count=1 < cap=2, allowed. Third at time 60: showings in last 60 min (from 0 to 60) include times 0 and 30, count=2 which equals cap, so not allowed (violation).

Time 10: count in last 20 min (from -10 to 10) is 0, allowed. Time 20: last 20 min (0 to 20) has showing at 10, count=1 < 3, allowed. Time 30: last 20 min (10 to 30) has showings at 10 and 20, count=2 < 3, allowed.
